label: ai
topic: Review of a psychological horror film with Hugh Grant as a sinister intellectual
text:
For the past decade, Hugh Grant has been undertaking one of the most fascinating career reinventions in modern cinema, trading the bumbling, floppy-haired romantic comedy leads of his youth for a series of delightfully slimy, eccentric character roles. Yet, nothing could have adequately prepared me for his terrifyingly charismatic turn in this latest psychological horror thriller. The premise is elegantly simple: two young Mormon missionaries knock on the wrong door and find themselves trapped in the sprawling, maze-like home of a sinister, fiercely intellectual recluse. What follows is a profoundly unsettling examination of faith, control, and theological manipulation that, despite a frustratingly conventional final act, easily earns three and a half out of five stars in my book.

The film's first half is an absolute masterclass in tension-building and psychological warfare. As a student currently surviving a grueling seminar on the philosophy of religion, I was utterly captivated by the script's razor-sharp dialogue. It operates as a deadly game of cat-and-mouse masquerading as polite theological debate. Grant plays the villainous homeowner with a warm, disarming intellect that makes him endlessly compelling to watch. Rather than relying on immediate physical threats, he weaponizes the missionaries' own doctrine against them, dissecting their carefully memorized talking points and trapping them in rhetorical corners long before he literally locks the physical doors.

There is a standout scene early in the film that I haven't been able to stop thinking about. The missionaries are sitting on Grant's plush velvet sofa, sharing a slice of blueberry pie, as he casually compares the evolution of global religions to the various iterations of the board game Monopoly. It is a brilliant, dizzying monologue, delivered with the cadence of an eccentric Oxford professor, yet dripping with a chillingly arrogant malice. As a viewer, I felt the exact same suffocating dread that the two young women were experiencing. The claustrophobia is palpable, aided by brilliant production design that makes the house feel like an intricate, inescapable puzzle box.

Unfortunately, the film struggles to maintain this dizzying intellectual high-wire act. As the narrative transitions from the cerebral first half into the third act, it abruptly discards its psychological nuance in favor of standard, run-of-the-mill horror conventions. The moment the characters descend into the hidden basement labyrinth, the film trades theological dread for typical slasher tropes. The intricate debates are replaced by breathless running, physical gore, and a series of highly improbable escapes. Sitting in the theater, I found myself profoundly disappointed as the terrifyingly brilliant antagonist was reduced to a standard movie monster, shouting generic villainous monologues as he chased the girls through the dark.

Despite this disappointing tonal shift in the climax, the film remains a highly effective and thoroughly engaging piece of genre cinema. The two young actresses playing the missionaries hold their own incredibly well against Grant's towering performance, bringing a desperate, sympathetic humanity to their roles that grounds the high-minded philosophical debates. While I wish the director had possessed the courage to follow the intellectual horror through to its natural, quieter conclusion, the sheer brilliance of the first hour cannot be denied. It is a deeply uncomfortable, thought-provoking thriller that will make you think twice before ever knocking on a stranger's door, and it unequivocally secures Grant's status as one of our most capable modern character actors.
